text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Cycle 2 of Ipilimumab/Nivolumab -- Treatment Tolerance and Response Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Marcus Ellison, a 56-year-old male with Stage IV BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, presents for his scheduled follow-up visit following Cycle 2 of combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab and nivolumab. He is being seen approximately 6 weeks after initiation of his second-line systemic regimen. He reports overall tolerance that is manageable, though not without side effects.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Ellison was initially diagnosed with Stage IV (M1c) metastatic melanoma arising from a 6.1 mm ulcerated nodular melanoma on the upper back, with bilateral axillary and left cervical nodal involvement, multiple hepatic metastases, and widespread subcutaneous satellite nodules. His tumor harbored a BRAF V600E mutation confirmed on comprehensive molecular profiling, and he was started on dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg twice daily plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg once daily as first-line therapy given his high hepatic disease burden and markedly elevated LDH at presentation (642 U/L). He had a rapid and gratifying initial response -- at 6 weeks his palpable subcutaneous deposits had largely melted away, and his LDH normalized; restaging CT at 3 months confirmed excellent partial response with the right axillary mass down by roughly 60% and the main liver lesions down by two-thirds. There was a rough plateau through the 6-month scan. Unfortunately, his 8-month CT demonstrated unmistakable progression with near-doubling of the axillary mass, marked enlargement of the hepatic lesions, two new hepatic deposits, new subcutaneous nodules, and a new right external iliac node -- along with LDH back up to 589 U/L. He was counseled extensively about acquired BRAF/MEK inhibitor resistance; repeat biopsy of a new subcutaneous abdominal nodule confirmed metastatic melanoma with acquired BRAF amplification and a MEK1 p.P124S resistance mutation, as well as an emergent PIK3CA E545K mutation on next-generation sequencing.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board review and brain MRI confirming no intracranial involvement, Mr. Ellison was started on second-line combination immunotherapy with ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV and nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks, dosed per the CheckMate 067 regimen with four planned induction cycles to be followed by nivolumab 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance. He received Cycle 1 approximately 6 weeks ago without significant acute infusion-related events. Cycle 2 was administered approximately 3 weeks ago, again without acute reactions. He presents today for routine treatment monitoring and toxicity assessment prior to proceeding with Cycle 3.

Since starting immunotherapy, the patient reports the following:
- Fatigue: present and somewhat bothersome, grade 1-2 in severity. He describes feeling more tired than usual, particularly in the afternoons, but is still able to carry out basic activities of daily living and has been going for short walks. He does not feel incapacitated.
- Skin: Mild itchiness over the trunk and arms, grade 1. He notes a new fine, pinkish maculopapular rash on his anterior chest and upper back that appeared about 2 weeks ago. He has been applying over-the-counter hydrocortisone cream with partial relief. No blistering, no mucosal involvement, no skin sloughing.
- Gastrointestinal: He notes loosening of his stools over the past week -- going from his baseline of one formed bowel movement daily to approximately 2-3 loose (not watery) stools per day. No blood in stool, no mucus, no urgency, no nocturnal diarrhea. No significant abdominal cramping at this time. No nausea or vomiting. He is eating and drinking without difficulty.
- He denies fever or chills.
- He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations.
- He denies headache, visual changes, confusion, focal neurologic symptoms.
- He denies joint pains or myalgias beyond the general fatigue.
- He denies dysuria or hematuria.
- He denies any oral ulcers.

On clinical examination, and comparing to his exam at the time of starting immunotherapy, there appears to be some early reduction in the palpable soft tissue disease. The right axillary region is still palpable but appears somewhat smaller and softer than at baseline. This is an early and encouraging sign, particularly given that immunotherapy-mediated responses tend to lag behind targeted therapy responses and can take 3-4 months to manifest clearly on imaging.

Laboratory results reviewed today are notable for LDH of 412 U/L -- this represents a meaningful decline from the pre-immunotherapy baseline of 589 U/L, consistent with early biologic response. LFTs remain mildly elevated (AST and ALT in the 70-80 range, consistent with residual hepatic disease burden rather than hepatitis; the trend will be followed). CBC is unremarkable.

Overall, the picture is encouraging: early clinical and biochemical evidence of immune activation and early tumor response, in the context of manageable but real immune-related toxicity.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue (grade 1-2), no fever, no rigors, no weight loss (weight stable at approximately 78 kg).
Skin: Grade 1 pruritus diffuse; grade 1 maculopapular rash on trunk, partially responding to topical steroids. No new or changing skin lesions referred to dermatology.
Gastrointestinal: 2-3 loose stools per day (grade 1 diarrhea, increase over baseline). No hematochezia, no melena, no mucoid stool, no nocturnal episodes. No nausea or vomiting. Appetite is preserved and improved compared to nadir during progression.
Pulmonary: No dyspnea, no cough, no pleurisy.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
Neurologic: No headache, no confusion, no focal deficits, no vision changes.
Musculoskeletal: No new joint swelling or myalgias.
Endocrine: No symptoms of thyroid dysfunction; specifically no heat/cold intolerance, no palpitations, no constipation or diarrhea beyond what is described above.
Genitourinary: Normal urine output, no dysuria.
Hematologic: No bruising, no bleeding.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 122/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 37.1 degrees C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight 78 kg (stable).

General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Mood appears engaged and cautiously optimistic.

Skin: Fine maculopapular rash distributed over the anterior chest and upper back, approximately 15-20% body surface area, non-confluent, non-vesicular. Areas of mild xerosis on bilateral forearms and lower legs. Erythematous patches on bilateral palms consistent with residual hyperkeratosis from prior dabrafenib therapy, improving. Prior right forearm excision site for cuSCC: well-healed scar, no recurrence noted. No new suspicious pigmented lesions on cursory exam; formal dermatology surveillance scheduled.

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Extraocular movements intact. No scleralicterus. Oral mucosa moist and intact, no ulcerations or thrush.

Neck: Supple. Residual left cervical lymphadenopathy: left level II lymph node palpable at approximately 1.0-1.2 cm, softer and smaller compared to initial presentation. No new cervical or supraclavicular adenopathy.

Chest/Pulmonary: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Good air movement throughout.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. Mild right upper quadrant fullness on deep palpation, consistent with known hepatic disease; no frank hepatomegaly palpable on standard exam. No tenderness to palpation diffusely. No peritoneal signs. Prior biopsy site on right abdomen is well-healed.

Lymph Nodes: Right axillary region -- firm but somewhat reduced soft tissue fullness compared to initial baseline; compared to the scan at progression (3.4 cm mass), this feels smaller, estimated clinically at perhaps 2.0-2.5 cm equivalent, though formal imaging will be needed for accurate assessment. Left axillary: no significant adenopathy. Inguinal: no palpable adenopathy bilaterally.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Peripheral pulses intact.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No ataxia. Gait normal.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 7.2 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.8 g/dL (mild anemia, grade 1; down from 12.2 g/dL at immunotherapy start -- likely multifactorial, chronic inflammation, prior therapy effect)
- Platelets: 218 x10^9/L (normal)
- Neutrophils: 4.8 x10^9/L, Lymphocytes: 1.6 x10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138, Potassium 4.0, Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, BUN 14 mg/dL
- AST 76 U/L (mildly elevated -- attributed to hepatic metastatic involvement; compare to 94 U/L at immunotherapy baseline -- slight improvement)
- ALT 71 U/L (mildly elevated, similar trajectory)
- Total bilirubin 1.0 mg/dL (stable, normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase 98 U/L (high-normal)

LDH: 412 U/L -- notable decline from 589 U/L at the start of immunotherapy; still elevated above upper limit of normal, but the downward trajectory is clinically meaningful and consistent with early tumor response.

TSH: 2.8 mIU/L (normal; baseline thyroid function being tracked given known immune-mediated thyroid toxicity risk with checkpoint inhibitors).

CRP: 18 mg/L (mildly elevated; non-specific; no fever or signs of active infection).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging has been obtained since the pre-immunotherapy brain MRI (which showed no CNS involvement) and the CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed at the time of BRAF/MEK inhibitor progression (right axillary mass 3.4 cm, hepatic lesions enlarged, new lesions present). Formal restaging imaging is not yet due at this early 6-week timepoint and will be ordered ahead of the next clinic visit. Clinical and biochemical parameters are being used as interim response surrogates.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IV (M1c) BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic melanoma, on second-line combination immunotherapy (ipilimumab/nivolumab, CheckMate 067 dosing), status post Cycle 2 of 4 induction cycles:

The patient is at an early but promising juncture in his immunotherapy course. LDH has dropped by over 30% from the pre-treatment baseline, and clinical examination suggests some early softening and reduction of the right axillary disease. The rash and loose stools are expected early signals of immune activation -- the same immune effectors that produce these toxicities are likely also attacking the tumor. We discussed with Mr. Ellison that immunotherapy responses often take longer to manifest radiographically than targeted therapy responses, and that the first restaging CT will be obtained around the 3-month mark. He is encouraged by his own clinical sense of improvement and the falling LDH.

Plan:
- Proceed with Cycle 3 of ipilimumab/nivolumab on schedule today, pending labs reviewed and toxicity assessment confirming safety to treat. Labs as reviewed above do not reveal any hold criteria (no grade 3+ hepatitis, no significant cytopenias, no electrolyte abnormalities).
- Pre-medicate with antiemetics (ondansetron 8 mg IV) and routine supportive care per nursing protocol.
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast planned for approximately 6 weeks from now (approximately 3 months from cycle 1), with interval brain MRI.

2. Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) -- skin and gastrointestinal:

Grade 1 maculopapular rash: Continue topical hydrocortisone cream (1%) twice daily to affected areas. Patient instructed to avoid hot showers, use fragrance-free moisturizers, and protect from direct sun exposure. No systemic steroids indicated at grade 1. Dermatology referral for baseline full-body skin exam also to cover ongoing monitoring for new cuSCC given prior BRAF inhibitor-related squamous cell carcinoma on the right forearm.

Grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day above baseline): This is below the threshold requiring treatment hold. The patient has been counseled extensively about the signs and symptoms of immune-mediated colitis, which can develop suddenly and escalate. I explained clearly: if stools increase to 4 or more per day above baseline, if there is any blood in the stool, severe cramping, fever, or dehydration, he is to call the on-call oncology nurse immediately and proceed to the emergency department if symptoms are severe. He should not wait for a scheduled appointment.
- Initiated formal stool diary: patient to log daily bowel movement frequency, consistency, and any associated symptoms.
- Loperamide 2 mg orally after each loose stool (maximum 8 mg/day) offered for symptomatic relief; patient agreeable.
- Dietary counseling: low-residue diet encouraged (avoid high-fiber foods, raw vegetables, dairy for now), increase fluid intake.
- Low threshold for urgent GI evaluation and possible sigmoidoscopy/colonoscopy if diarrhea worsens.
- Stool cultures (C. diff PCR, bacterial pathogens, ova and parasites) ordered as baseline in the event of escalation requiring corticosteroid use.
- Current diarrhea does NOT require a treatment hold; will re-assess at cycle 3 visit and at next follow-up.

3. Immune-related hypothyroidism monitoring:

TSH is within normal limits today at 2.8 mIU/L. No intervention required. Will monitor at every other cycle. Patient counseled that immune-mediated thyroid dysfunction can develop at any point during immunotherapy, often presenting with fatigue (which is non-specific), and that routine thyroid monitoring is essential. Signs of hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, weight gain) and hyperthyroidism (palpitations, tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss) reviewed.

4. Mild anemia (grade 1, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL):

Likely multifactorial: chronic disease, prior cytotoxic insult from prior dabrafenib/trametinib therapy, early immune activation. No reticulocytopenia or hemolytic features on smear review. Will monitor at each cycle. Dietary iron and folate encouraged. No transfusion needed or anticipated at current hemoglobin.

5. Ongoing mild elevation of hepatic transaminases (AST 76, ALT 71 U/L):

These values are stable-to-mildly improving from the immunotherapy baseline (AST 94, ALT 78 at start) and are most consistent with residual hepatic metastatic involvement rather than immune-mediated hepatitis. Grades 1 transaminase elevation does not require treatment hold per guidelines. Will continue to monitor carefully -- the pattern to watch for would be a rising AST/ALT not explained by tumor progression, rising bilirubin, or jaundice, which would raise concern for checkpoint inhibitor-related hepatitis. Current values: no hold criteria met.

6. Prior cuSCC right forearm (managed with excision approximately 4 months ago):

Dermatology surveillance ongoing. No recurrence noted on exam today. Dermatology follow-up appointment confirmed.

7. Performance status and quality of life:

ECOG PS: 1. Patient reports that despite fatigue, he feels notably better than he did in the weeks preceding immunotherapy start, when rapid hepatic and nodal progression was causing abdominal discomfort and significant malaise. He has a positive attitude. Psychosocial: patient coping well; offered social work referral, patient declined at this time but is aware of the resource.

8. Return to clinic / next steps:

- Cycle 3 ipilimumab 3 mg/kg IV + nivolumab 1 mg/kg IV administered today after this visit, pending pharmacy preparation.
- Next clinic visit: 3 weeks (prior to Cycle 4).
- Restaging CT scheduled for approximately 3 months from cycle 1 (to coincide with post-induction assessment and transition planning to nivolumab maintenance).
- Brain MRI to be ordered for the same time point.
- Repeat TSH at next visit in 3 weeks.
- CBC and CMP at next visit.
- Continue stool diary; return precautions discussed and reinforced in writing.
- Levothyroxine dose will be held (patient not yet on thyroid replacement; current TSH normal); reassess at next visit.

Patient verbalized understanding of the treatment plan, return precautions, and warning signs requiring urgent contact. He expressed a desire to continue treatment and remains motivated. Questions were answered. Time spent in counseling and coordination: approximately 35 minutes of a 60-minute visit.

Electronically signed,
Attending Medical Oncologist, Melanoma Program
